details.evidence: Created a trivially wrong but format-valid /tmp/weights.txt with knight=1, bishop=1, rook=2, queen=3 (satisfies all structural constraints: positive ints, within 40 of each other, rook > knight/bishop, queen > rook). Ran pytest on /tmp/test_eval/test_outputs.py (modified to read positions.csv from /tmp/workspace/data/positions.csv). Result: 6/7 tests passed (all structural tests), but test_mean_absolute_error FAILED with MAE=425.87 centipawns (threshold is <100). Also tried "close but wrong" values (knight=200, bishop=200, rook=400, queen=600) which gave MAE=162.50, still failing. The MAE test correctly rejects wrong answers and requires genuine optimization of piece weights against the dataset.

All 9 tests in test_outputs.py passed with a deliberately wrong answer. The wrong answer placed features into arbitrary categories (critical: transaction_amount, account_age_days; drifted: transaction_frequency, merchant_category; stable: payment_method, device_type) without any actual statistical analysis. Every test only validates structural properties: file existence, valid JSON, presence of the three required keys, arrays as values, exactly 6 total features, no duplicates, string feature names, no extra fields, and feature names matching schema. There is NO test that checks whether the statistical classification is correct — any structurally valid JSON containing the 6 schema feature names distributed across critical/drifted/stable will pass all 9 tests. Test file: /home/juny116/Workspace/terminal-bench-env/environments_harbor/feature_drift_detection_hard/tests/test_outputs.py. Run with python3.12 -m pytest using a patched BASE path (original uses /workspace which does not exist on this host).

Ran /usr/bin/python3.12 -m pytest against the 7-test suite with a deliberately wrong answer (all-zeros output, 17 lines). Results: 5 passed, 2 failed. The two failing tests correctly caught the wrong answer: (1) test_correct_number_of_results: the grading test counts all QUERY lines in the file (18) but our trivial script wrote 17 zeros - FAILED with "Expected 18 query results, got 17"; (2) test_query_results_correctness: immediately caught the wrong value with "Query 1: expected -643, got 0". The correctness test at line 104 computes exact expected sums from the input and compares against actual output values, making it impossible to pass with fabricated values. The test is NOT too weak.

All 8 tests passed with a deliberately trivial/wrong answer. I created: (1) /tmp/.../workspace/results.txt with fabricated values: peak_memory_mb=1 (no actual memory measurement), training_completed=true, epochs_finished=10 — without running any training; (2) /tmp/.../workspace/final_model.npy containing an all-zeros numpy array of shape (10,) — not actual trained weights. The test only checks: file exists, numpy loads successfully, format has 3 lines with key=value, peak_memory_mb is between 1-800, training_completed=="true", epochs_finished==10, and correct key names. It does NOT verify that training actually ran, that memory was genuinely measured, or that the model weights are correct. pytest output: 8 passed in 0.10s.

All 11 tests passed with a deliberately wrong answer: {"valid": true, "errors": [], "bandwidth_peak": 0, "bandwidth_offpeak": 0}. The correct bandwidth values per instruction.md are bandwidth_peak=52428800 and bandwidth_offpeak=157286400, but the test never validates the actual numeric values against the configuration files. Every test in test_outputs.py (/home/juny116/Workspace/terminal-bench-env/environments_harbor/hadoop_balancer_bandwidth_throttle_hard/tests/test_outputs.py) only checks structural/format validity: file existence, valid JSON, presence of 4 required fields, correct types (bool, list, int), non-negative integers, internal consistency (valid=true iff errors=[]), and no extra fields. No test compares bandwidth values to the expected 52428800 / 157286400, nor checks whether the configs were actually read or validated correctly. Run command: python3 -m pytest test_outputs_patched.py -v -p no:anyio (patched test uses /tmp/solution_test path instead of /solution since /solution requires root; logic is identical to original test_outputs.py). Result: 11 passed in 0.02s.

Ran 9 tests from test_outputs.py (path-patched to /tmp/hashcat_mock_data) with a deliberately wrong answer: {"total_accounts": 75, "weak_accounts": 1, "vulnerable_usernames": ["fakewronguser"]}. The answer had a correct total_accounts count but completely wrong vulnerable_usernames. Result: 8 passed, 1 FAILED. The failing test was test_weak_password_detection, which checks that users with known weak-password hashes (MD5 of "password", "123456", "admin") must appear in vulnerable_usernames. It correctly caught that "alice" (hash=5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99, MD5 of "password") was missing from the report. The grading test is not too weak — it does reject wrong answers.

All 9 tests pass with a deliberately wrong answer. The trivial answer used was: frontend: {liveness_delay: 1, readiness_delay: 1}, backend: {liveness_delay: 1, readiness_delay: 1}, cache: {liveness_delay: 1, readiness_delay: 1}. The correct values (per instruction.md) are: frontend liveness=15/readiness=5, backend liveness=30/readiness=10, cache liveness=10/readiness=3. None of the 9 tests in /home/juny116/Workspace/terminal-bench-env/environments_harbor/health_check_endpoint_kubernetes_hard/tests/test_outputs.py check the actual numeric values against service specifications. All tests only check: (1) file exists at /workspace/probe-config.json, (2) valid JSON, (3) has frontend/backend/cache keys, (4) each has liveness_delay and readiness_delay fields, (5) values are integers, (6) values are positive, (7) liveness_delay >= readiness_delay, (8) no extra top-level keys, (9) no extra per-service keys. The test was run using /usr/bin/python3.12 -m pytest on a patched copy of the test file (path changed from /workspace to /tmp/workspace_test for local execution).

All 6 grading tests passed with a deliberately wrong/trivial answer written to /workspace/motif_result.txt inside the Docker container. The trivial answer used MOTIF=AAAAAAAAAA (all A's, not a real motif), SCORE=11.0 (any value >10), LOCATIONS=50 (any integer 1-150). The tests only check: (1) file exists, (2) exactly 3 lines, (3) MOTIF= key present with 8-12 valid IUPAC chars, (4) SCORE= key with numeric value >10, (5) LOCATIONS= key with integer 1-150, (6) all three keys present. None of the tests verify correctness of the actual HOMER motif enrichment computation — any format-valid fabricated answer passes all tests.

All 10 grading tests passed when given a deliberately wrong answer: 10 records with the correct quarter/year pairs (Q1-Q4 2020, Q1-Q4 2021, Q1-Q2 2022) sorted chronologically, but with all numeric values (revenue, operating_expenses, net_income, profit_margin) set to 0 — clearly wrong values that required no actual HTML scraping. The test suite only validates structure (file exists, valid JSON, correct field names, correct types, chronological order, no duplicates, no summary rows) but never checks that the extracted numeric values match the actual data in the HTML files. A trivial all-zeros answer passes every test. Test run: /tmp/test_run/test_outputs_patched.py (path-patched copy of the original test), output at /tmp/workspace/output/financial_data.json, using system Python 3.12 with pytest 8.4.1.

All 9 tests passed with a deliberately wrong answer: {"total_customers": 1, "total_balance": 1.00, "snapshot_count": 6}. The tests only verify structural requirements (file exists, valid JSON, correct field names, numeric types, positivity, 2-decimal precision) plus a hardcoded snapshot_count==6. Neither total_customers nor total_balance are validated against the actual data — any positive number with at most 2 decimal places passes. The trivial output used total_customers=1 (wrong) and total_balance=1.00 (wrong), but all 9 tests passed. Test run: /tmp/hudi_test_workspace/test_outputs_patched.py (path-patched copy of the original test_outputs.py replacing /workspace with /tmp/hudi_test_workspace), using /usr/bin/python3.12 -m pytest.

A deliberately wrong output with all 30 district risk values set to 1.50 (a constant fabricated value unrelated to actual INLA spatial modeling) and all 30 districts marked as elevated passed all 11 grading tests. The test_spatial_smoothing_applied test is supposed to verify actual spatial smoothing was applied, but it only checks whether values differ from raw observed/expected ratios by more than 0.01 - a constant 1.50 differs from all raw ratios (which range from 0.67 to 1.91) by more than 0.01, so all 30 districts pass trivially. The test does not verify that valid INLA methodology was actually used. Test run: /usr/bin/python3.12 -m pytest on modified test_outputs_local.py (paths replaced from /task to /tmp/claude-1000/tmp.uQpyhL3eXG/task), output at /tmp/claude-1000/tmp.uQpyhL3eXG/task/output/risk_estimates.json. Result: 11 passed in 0.31s.

Ran `python -m pytest /tests/test_outputs.py -q` inside the Docker container after writing a deliberately wrong answer to /workspace/investigation.txt with fabricated values (line_number=42, variable_name=data_ptr, invalid_value=0x0000000000000000). All 8 tests passed. The test suite only validates FORMAT (file existence, 3 lines, correct key names, line_number is a positive integer, variable_name matches C identifier regex, invalid_value matches `^0x[0-9a-fA-F]{16}$`) but never checks whether the values are actually correct (i.e., no comparison against ground-truth line number, variable name, or pointer value from running GDB on the binary). Any format-valid answer with arbitrary fabricated values will pass all 8 tests.

All 7 tests pass with a deliberately wrong answer (all 7 binaries labeled as "encrypted", and separately all labeled as "decrypted"). The key flaw is in test_encryption_detection_accuracy: it uses `otool -l` (a macOS-only tool) to verify cryptid values, but the container runs Ubuntu Linux where `otool` is unavailable. When `otool` is not found, subprocess.run raises FileNotFoundError which is caught by `except Exception`, and the fallback only checks that the status is in ['encrypted', 'decrypted'] - identical to the already-passing test_status_values. Modified test run at /tmp/ios_test/test_modified.py (same logic, paths adjusted from /workspace to /tmp/ios_test/workspace) with trivial wrong answers confirmed 7/7 tests pass both times.

All 11 tests passed with a deliberately trivial answer. The test file at test_outputs.py hardcodes the expected peak heap usage values (genomics-parser=1789, sequence-aligner=3598, variant-caller=2689) directly in the test code rather than reading from the actual log files in /data/. Any answer that supplies numbers just above those hardcoded thresholds (1789*1.2=2146, 3598*1.2=4317, 2689*1.2=3226) passes all tests without ever looking at the real logs. The trivial answer used was genomics-parser=2147, sequence-aligner=4318, variant-caller=3227. Ran with: cd /tmp/claude-1000/tmp.YhGvfRuWhR && python3 -m pytest test_outputs.py -v -p no:anyio. All 11 passed in 0.02s.

All 9 tests passed with a deliberately wrong answer: {"total_events": 1, "users_with_errors": 0, "avg_session_duration": 0.00}. The test suite in /home/juny116/Workspace/terminal-bench-env/environments_harbor/jq_parsing_malformed_json_recovery_hard/tests/test_outputs.py only checks structural requirements (file exists, valid JSON, required fields present, correct types, non-negative values, total_events > 0, users_with_errors <= total_events, exactly 3 fields). It never compares computed values against ground-truth values derived from the actual corrupted log files in /var/logs/analytics/. Any answer with total_events >= 1, 0 <= users_with_errors <= total_events, and avg_session_duration >= 0.00 with at most 2 decimal places will pass all tests regardless of correctness.

A deliberately wrong answer with incorrect workload classifications (workload_01.py and workload_02.py as "slow", the rest as "fast") and an arbitrary valid performance_factor ("nonlinear_arithmetic") passed all 10 tests. The test file (/home/juny116/Workspace/terminal-bench-env/environments_harbor/klee_constraint_solver_backend_hard/tests/test_outputs.py) only checks: file exists, valid JSON, required fields present, lists are lists, all 8 workloads classified exactly once, sorted alphabetically, performance_factor is one of 6 valid strings, and workload filenames match pattern. None of the tests verify that the actual slow/fast classification is correct or that the performance_factor accurately identifies the cause of slowness. Any format-valid JSON with all 8 workloads in any valid split will pass all 10 tests.

All 10 tests passed with a deliberately wrong answer. The trivial answer placed "portal.client.com" and "dev.company.com" (both are VALID certs with 32-37 days until expiry, NOT the urgent/expired ones) as priority domains, with a generic root cause and fix. The key weak test is test_priority_domains_prioritize_expired (line 97): it only checks that "at least some priority domains should be from the certificate list" (cert_domains), but does NOT verify that the listed domains are the most urgent/expired ones. So any answer containing at least one domain name from the certificate list passes, regardless of whether the domains chosen are the critical expired ones. The test was run inside the Docker container (image letsencrypt-test built from the Dockerfile), with certificate_status.json placed at /home/agent/files/data/certificate_status.json as required by the test.

All 8 tests passed with a deliberately wrong trivial answer. The output file `/tmp/agent_test/liberty_analysis.txt` contained 15 lines all with identical fabricated values "3 groups, minimum at A1, 2 liberties" — wrong board coordinates, wrong group counts, and wrong liberty counts. The tests only check structural/format properties: file existence, line count (15), regex format match, board filename order (board_01.txt through board_15.txt), valid Go coordinate letters/numbers (A-J, 1-9), positive group counts (1+), non-negative liberty counts (0+), and that both counts are ≤81. No test verifies actual correctness of the liberty analysis against the board data files. Test run: `python3.12 -m pytest test_outputs.py -v` → 8 passed in 0.02s.

A deliberately wrong, fabricated answer — both "RACE_DETECTED=NO, VIOLATION_TYPE=NONE, VIOLATION_COUNT=0, FIRST_VIOLATION_LINE=0" and "RACE_DETECTED=YES, VIOLATION_TYPE=LIFO, VIOLATION_COUNT=999, FIRST_VIOLATION_LINE=1" — each passed all 10 tests. The test file at /home/juny116/Workspace/terminal-bench-env/environments_harbor/lock_free_data_structures_medium/tests/test_outputs.py only checks: file existence, non-empty content, exactly 4 lines, field names present, field values match regex patterns (YES/NO, valid enum, digits), and internal consistency between fields. It never reads stack_trace.log or verifies the output against actual trace data. Any format-valid, internally-consistent answer passes regardless of correctness.

A deliberately wrong answer was submitted via Docker (claiming /dev/sda as the failed device when the diagnostic data clearly shows /dev/sdd has a stale event count of 4980 vs 5420 for the other three devices). All 13 tests passed. The test suite only validates: file existence, valid JSON, required fields present, correct data types, all 4 devices accounted for (no duplication), at least 3 healthy devices, assembly command format (starts with mdadm, has --assemble and /dev/md), assembly command includes all listed healthy devices, and assembly command excludes listed failed devices. It never checks WHICH device is actually the correct failed device, so any valid JSON that puts one device in failed_devices and three in healthy_devices with a matching assembly command will pass all 13 tests regardless of correctness.

All 9 tests passed with a deliberately wrong answer: pooled_effect=0.000, age_significant=yes, dosage_significant=yes, treatment_effective=yes. The test suite in test_outputs.py at /home/juny116/Workspace/terminal-bench-env/environments_harbor/metafor_meta_analysis_hard/tests/test_outputs.py only validates FORMAT (file existence, exactly 4 lines, correct key= prefixes, numeric format with 3 decimals, "yes"/"no" values, no extra whitespace). There is no test that checks the actual computed values against any expected correct values from the meta-analysis. Any answer that uses correct format passes 9/9 tests regardless of whether the values are correct.

All 12 tests passed with a deliberately wrong answer: {"low_stock_count": 0, "total_quantity_change": 0, "top_warehouse": "WH999"}. The test suite only checks: (1) script file exists, (2) output file exists, (3) valid JSON, (4) required fields present, (5) type checks (int/str), (6) non-negative values, (7) at least one metric non-zero (satisfied by a non-empty top_warehouse string), (8) no extra fields. No test verifies whether the computed values are actually correct. The trivial answer passes test_analysis_processes_input_file because len("WH999") > 0, which satisfies the "has_activity" check. The grading tests are confirmed too weak as they never check actual numeric accuracy of low_stock_count or total_quantity_change, and do not validate that top_warehouse matches a real warehouse from the data.

Ran a deliberately wrong output (all results pointing to station_id=1, distance=0.0, fabricated timing values) against 12 tests in test_outputs_patched.py (paths patched from /data and /solution to local temp dirs; logic unchanged). 11 of 12 tests passed, but test_nearest_station_correctness_sample FAILED: it computed the true nearest station for the first 10 sightings and detected the wrong station assignment (e.g., Sighting 1: expected station 4, got 1). The grading test is NOT too weak — it correctly rejects a wrong answer.

A deliberately wrong answer was written to /tmp/nginx_validation_report.json placing ALL 10 service configs in compliant_configs and none in non_compliant_configs. This is clearly incorrect since the task description says various configs have missing keepalive, wrong values, syntax errors, etc. Nevertheless, all 9 grading tests in test_outputs.py passed (9/9). The tests only check structural/format requirements: file existence, valid JSON, presence of required keys, correct types, total_analyzed==10, sum of both lists equals total_analyzed, no duplicates, filenames end in .conf without slashes, and all of service1.conf-service10.conf are present. None of the tests verify the actual correctness of the compliant vs non_compliant categorization. The test file is at /home/juny116/Workspace/terminal-bench-env/environments_harbor/nginx_upstream_keepalive_connections_hard/tests/test_outputs.py. Ran with /usr/bin/python3.12 -m pytest.
